Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. At 29, I've got a lot going on outside of school, so I'd usually just fit studying in whenever I could rather than having a set schedule. I'd listen to lectures while commuting or doing chores since I learn better by hearing things anyway. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything - it really depended on what else was happening in my life.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on deadlines and whatever else was going on. I'd have these bursts of activity when something was due, then kind of drift for a while. Regular schedules have never been my thing - I've tried before but life always gets in the way. With 15 online courses, I was constantly juggling and just reacting to whatever was most urgent.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about something due would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance wasn't great. Occasionally I'd go in because I remembered something from a lecture I wanted to revisit. It wasn't really a habit, more like responding to pressure.
Q4: For a brief period, I did get into a rhythm of checking the LMS most days, usually in the morning with my coffee. That happened when I was doing better with the mindfulness exercises they recommended - it helped with my anxiety and made me feel more in control. But that routine fell apart when things got busy, and I never quite got back to it consistently.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the information that way. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in different ways. I spent about an hour watching videos when I did engage, and that's where most of my actual learning happened.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. Reading on a screen just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. I'd also skip a lot of the optional practice activities because I was always behind on the required stuff anyway. If it wasn't going to directly impact my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to passing the course or understanding something I was struggling with. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. I also valued things that were efficient - I don't have time to waste on busywork at this point in my life.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my completion rate already not great, I had to prioritize what was actually required. Required activities came with consequences, so they got my attention even when I didn't feel like doing them. Optional stuff just fell off my radar entirely most of the time.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download audio versions of lectures or record myself reading notes aloud so I could study while doing other things. I used a lot of external podcasts and YouTube videos that explained the same concepts differently. The LMS only shows part of my studying because a lot of it happened through my earbuds while I was living my life.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd listen to downloaded lectures on repeat during my commute or while cooking dinner. I'd also have study conversations with a friend who was taking similar courses - we'd talk through concepts on the phone. None of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it maybe three or four more times offline over the next week. The LMS would just show one access, but I was actually engaging with that material repeatedly. That's just how I learn best - repetition through listening, not clicking through screens.
Q12: My anxiety would spike sometimes, especially around midterms or when multiple deadlines piled up. Even though I'm generally resilient and my stress is manageable, the anxiety could make it hard to even start working on things. The mindfulness intervention helped a lot with that - it gave me tools to calm down and actually engage instead of just panicking.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was overwhelmed with work or life stuff, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Fatigue was a big factor too - after a long day, the last thing I wanted was more screen time. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later kept getting pushed back. My completion rates definitely reflect that pattern.
Q14: Oh yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, technically watching a video or scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. Those sessions were basically useless even though they probably looked like engagement in the data.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was anxiety - starting felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it. Part of it was just that other things felt more urgent in the moment. I'm good at bouncing back, so I'd always tell myself I could handle it later, which worked out okay but wasn't ideal.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage. Without that pressure, I'd drift. My LMS activity probably looks like a series of spikes right before due dates with valleys in between. It's not the healthiest pattern, but it got me through.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but I wasn't always quick to act on it. Positive feedback made me feel like maybe I was doing okay despite everything. Critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse, which paradoxically made me avoid engaging more. The delay in my response time probably shows that - I needed time to process before acting.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because without rigid structure, I'd let things slide. More frequent smaller deadlines might have kept me on track better than having big assignments spread out. I needed more external scaffolding than the course provided.
Q19: Having video lectures was huge for me as an auditory learner - that made engagement much easier. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long reading documents. The discussion requirements felt awkward too; I posted my five contributions but they felt forced. More audio-based options would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and keeps checking things without actually submitting work - that's partially me. You might be logging in, reviewing materials, second-guessing yourself, but never feeling ready to turn something in. High activity doesn't mean productive activity; sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting as clicking around.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I passed the course despite my mediocre attendance and completion rates because I was actually learning through listening and repetition outside the system. The LMS activity is just the tip of the iceberg for some of us.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, how long you're logged in. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I actually understood something or just had the page open. They definitely miss all the offline studying and the emotional experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively listening, making connections, maybe talking through ideas with someone. It's mental presence, not just physical or digital presence. Some of my most engaged moments happened nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my anxiety and stay in the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing other things. It can't see that despite looking like a disengaged student on paper, I was actually fighting pretty hard to make this work around everything else in my life.
